feedback.text: I strongly support this initiative, having a first hand experience of the effects of ads and local websites with dark patterns, influencers strongly promoting online gambling to either minors or very young adults, often even without proper promotion disclosure, and the rise of harmful live service practices in videogames that potentially aim to coax minors into obsessively spend. In this act I would love to see a stronger emphasis on consumer protection regarding live service software/videogames and software which customers have only a seeming ownership of. In the digital landscape, software distribution is heavily brought up through purchasing licenses for a digital copy instead of an actual purchase of the copy, which is often disclosed with legally heavy and extremely elongated terms of service or not transparently disclosed at all. My concern is that customers are slowly losing their right to actually own digital copies of videogames and other software products through this and I think it would be very important for both software preservation and consumer interest if those types of issues are thoroughly looked at! Examples include games like Ubisoft's blockbuster title The Crew which was completely removed from user libraries after it's live service expiration, even though it was a purchased digital good, platforms like Steam not overtly saying you're only purchasing a license for a game and things like live service game providers being capable of completely suspending your account for any reason, even if a substantial amount of money was spent on goods within the game itself. Additionally, I would like some security around digital goods that are heavily produced using generative AI. Consumers have the right to know what has been produced by conscious effort and what is effortlessly AI generated and pushed as a product. Examples include widely sold AI generated books, art, music and video.
